segments.s265-g25: گزین کرد is a past compound verb ('chose'); the agent بیژن follows the verb (poetic postverbal subject). ز introduces the source 'from (the) army', and سوار is a noun 'riders/horsemen' specifying which part of the host was picked.
segments.s265-g26: دلیران و پرخاشجویان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the brave and the quarrelsome/combative' (plural -ان). هزار is the numeral 'thousand' quantifying that phrase; the number follows the described group in this poetic word order.
segments.s265-g27: رسیدند is past plural 'they arrived/came'; پس = 'then/afterwards'. یک به دیگر is a reciprocal phrase 'one to another' (they moved toward each other). فراز is an adverbial/locative 'on high / on the heights' placed after the phrase in poetic order.
segments.s265-g28: دو لشکر = 'two armies'. پر از is the fixed expression 'full of' (پر + از), taking a following noun phrase; کینه 'hatred/grudge' and رزمساز here describe the armies as filled with rancor and war‑making/instigation (i.e., eager for battle).
segments.s265-g29: همه 'all/everyone' is the universal subject; گرزها is the plural object 'maces'. برکشیدند is past plural 'they raised/lifted' (بر- + کشیدن). پاک here functions adverbially 'completely/utterly' modifying the action.
segments.s265-g30: یکی introduces 'one' or 'a certain' (here 'a'), followed by ابر 'cloud'. بست (past of بستن) here means 'closed/formed/covered' — the phrase says a cloud gathered or closed over. از بر is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/upon', and تیره خاک = 'dark dust/earth' where خاک is 'dust/earth' (dust here).
segments.s265-g31: فرود آمد is a two-part verbal idea 'came down/descended' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از کوه 'from the mountain' gives the source, and ابر سیاه 'the black cloud' (noun + adjective) is the thing that descends; poetic word order places the verb and source before the subject.
segments.s265-g32: بپوشید is past 'it covered/concealed' (پوشیدن 'to cover'); دیدار here functions as 'view/sight/scene' (poetic use related to meeting/seeing) and توران سپاه is a genitive/possessive grouping 'the army of Turan' (word order inverted for poetic effect), so the clause says the cloud covered the view of the Turanian host.
segments.s265-g89: This clause is a question in archaic word order: کرا (an old interrogative 'whom') is the object, followed by یابم, the 1st-person present/subjunctive form 'shall I find' or 'may I find'. اکنون 'now' is a time adverb. بدین رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'in/to this battlefield' (بدین = to/into this + رزمگاه 'battlefield') that locates the action; note the text splits بدین into separate tokens, but syntactically it functions as a single locative phrase modifying یابم.
segments.s265-g90: This clause gives the necessity clause that follows the question. به جنگ 'into/for battle' marks direction/goal; اندر آورد is a compound verbal idea 'bring in / bring into (the fight)' with آورد (to bring) as the main verb; باید 'must/it is necessary' expresses obligation and scopes over the clause; سپاه 'army' is the logical subject affected by the obligation (i.e., the army must be brought into battle). Word order here is somewhat inverted for poetry but reads as 'the army must be brought into battle.'
segments.s265-g91: Two coordinated verbal actions are compressed into one line: بزد 'he struck/sounded' takes نای رویین ('the iron reed/flute') as object; the conjunction و links a second verb phrase بربست کوس, where بربست is a past verb and کوس 'war-drum' is its object. Poetic diction permits unusual collocations (e.g., an 'iron' flute). The verbs are simple past actions presenting the opening signal of battle.
segments.s265-g92: This is a pair of verbless/nominal clauses linked by juxtaposition: هوا نیلگون شد literally 'the air became indigo' (نیلگون 'bluish/azure', شد 'became'), followed by زمین آبنوس where the copula is omitted but understood: 'the earth (became) ebony/very dark.' The verbless feeling is typical of epic poetry; شد supplies the change-of-state for the first noun phrase and is implied for the second.
segments.s265-g93: This is a poetic motion clause: ز کوه 'from the mountain' gives the source, کنابد is a present/past-poetic verb meaning 'toils/strains' (here conveying effort or laborious emergence), and برون شد 'came out/forth' places the action outward. سپاه is the subject 'the army/host' that issues forth. The verb کنابد adds the sense that the emergence is strenuous or as if the mountain labored to produce the host.
segments.s265-g94: بشد here functions as a perfective/past narrative 'it became/it happened' introducing روشنایی 'brightness/light' as the emergent phenomenon; ز خورشید و ماه 'from the sun and moon' gives the source of that light. The verb form is narrative and the clause means 'brightness came from the sun and moon.'
segments.s265-g95: سپهدار ایران 'the commander of Iran' is the nominative/title phrase; بزد is a simple past 'he struck/blew' and takes کرنای 'the trumpet/horn' as its object. The verb-object order (verb before object) is common in classical Persian narrative and here emphasises the commanding action that signals the army.
segments.s265-g96: This is a sequence of actions by the same subject: سپاه اندر آورد 'he brought the army in' (آورد = bring, اندر = into/among) states movement of the host, and the coordinating و links بگرفت جای 'and he seized/took the place' where بگرفت is a past verb 'seized' and جای 'place/position' is the object. The sequence shows command, mustering, and occupation as successive narrative events.
segments.s265-g33: سپهدار is the subject 'the commander' placed before the clause. چون introduces a temporal clause 'when/as' (poetic use). گرد تیره is a noun + adjective 'a dark cloud/ball of dust' (گرد here = dust), and بدید is a past tense verb 'saw' placed at the end of the clause in typical Persian order.
segments.s265-g34: کزو ('from which / from whom') refers back to the dark dust-cloud just mentioned. لشکر ترک = 'the Turk army/host' (ترک = Turkmen/Turks). شد ناپدید is a periphrastic description 'became vanished' (شد used as 'became' + adjectival ناپدید 'vanished') — a poetic/intransitive way to say the host disappeared.
segments.s265-g35: کمانها (bows) is the object/topic. بفرمود کردن combines an honorific/polite verb بفرمود (here 'ordered/commanded/caused') with the light verb کردن 'to do' to mean 'had (them) made/strung'. به زه literally 'to/with string' — i.e. 'to have the bows fitted with string' (strung). This is a causative/hortative construction common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s265-g36: برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth' and is placed before its noun خروش 'a cry/roar' (inversion is allowed in poetry). از مهان marks the source 'from the chiefs/guests/hosts'. و ز که is a coordinating fragment ('and from that/and from which') that links to whatever follows; که here functions as a relative/complementizer introducing the next idea.
segments.s265-g37: چو here means 'when' and introduces a temporal clause. بیژن is the subject. به نستیهن اندر uses به + place-name + اندر ('to Nestiḥan, into') — a doubled locative/directional expression common in classical Persian. رسید is the past tense 'arrived/entered' and ends the clause.
segments.s265-g38: درفش 'banner/standard' is the noun, followed by سر ویسگان — 'the chief/top of the Visgān' or 'the principal standard of the Visgān' (سر used as 'chief/principal'). را is the definite object marker marking the banner as the object, and بدید is the past 'saw'. Word order is object (with را) then verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s265-g39: هوا 'the air/atmosphere' is the subject. سر به سر گشته is an idiomatic/periphrastic phrase meaning 'has become entirely/throughout' (سر به سر = wholly/altogether) and گشته is the perfect/participial past 'has become/turned'. زنگارگون is an adjectival predicate 'rust-colored/tarnished'. The phrase order reflects poetic diction.
segments.s265-g40: زمین 'the ground/earth' is the subject here. شد is 'became'. به کردار is a simile formula 'in the manner of' or 'like', followed by دریای خون 'a sea of blood' (دریای = sea, خون = blood), so the whole clause means 'the earth became like a sea of blood'. This is a standard poetic simile construction.
segments.s265-g49: چنین is an adverb meaning 'thus/so' placed before the verb for emphasis; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here introducing reported speech); بیژن is the subject (proper name) that follows the verb by poetic inversion; به ایرانیان is a dative/indirect-object phrase 'to the Iranians'.
segments.s265-g50: که introduces a subordinate / relative clause ('that' or 'who'); هر کو is an older/poetic 'whoever' construction (هر = each/any, کو = archaic 'who'); ببندد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive ('binds'/'girds') used to express a general or potential action; کمر is the thing bound (here 'waist' or 'girdle' — 'to gird one's loins'); بر میان is a small prepositional phrase 'around the middle/waist' (بر = on/upon; میان = middle).
segments.s265-g51: بجز is a preposition 'except' marking an exception; گرز و شمشیر is a coordinated noun phrase 'mace and sword'; گیرد is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive ('takes'/'seizes') used here in a conditional/characterizing sense ('whoever takes'); به دست is a locative expression 'in hand' ('takes ... into his hand').
segments.s265-g52: کمان is the direct object 'a bow'; بر سرش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (سر = head; -ش = his); بر کنم is a first-person verb phrase 'I will put/place' where بر (repeated) encodes the direction 'upon' and کنم is the present/subjunctive/intentional form 'I do/make' used for a future/intended action; پاک پست is an adverb + adjective combination 'utterly low/abased' meaning 'completely abase/humble him.'
segments.s265-g53: که links this clause to the previous idea ('for' / 'that'); ترکان is the subject 'the Turks'; به دیدن is an instrumental/temporal phrase 'at the sight of / upon seeing'; پری چهره‌اند is a descriptive predicate: پری چهره (literally 'fairy-faced' = very beautiful) modifies چهره, and اند is the plural copula 'are' — together 'they are fairy-faced' applied to the object that attracts the Turks (i.e., 'at the sight of a fair woman').
segments.s265-g54: به جنگ here means 'in (matters of) war'; از introduces the domain of deprivation ('deprived of'); هنر is 'skill/art' (here specifically martial skill); پاک functions as an intensifier 'completely/altogether'; بی‌بهره‌اند is the predicate 'they are without / bereft of' (بی‌بهره = deprived, with اند copula).
segments.s265-g55: دلیری is the noun 'bravery/courage' placed before the verb for emphasis; گرفتند is the past plural 'they took/seized' — together 'they took courage' meaning 'they became brave'; کنداوران is the plural subject (a tribal/group name or designation) placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s265-g56: کشیدند is the past plural 'they drew/they pulled' used here in the sense 'they drew up' or 'they moved/advanced'; لشکر is the object 'army, host'; پرندآوران is the plural agent 'the bird-bringers' or 'winged (figurative) ones' — together 'the bird-bringing hosts drew up (their) army.' Note the verb–object–subject order is a poetic inversion.
segments.s265-g9: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. کارآگهان is a poetic compound meaning 'those aware / the informants' (agent). آگهی is the object 'news/information'. یافتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural 'they found/received' — together: 'when the informants received the news'.
segments.s265-g10: سبک here is used adverbially 'lightly / swiftly'. سوی + proper name gives direction 'toward Goudarz'. بشتافتند is a literary past plural 'they hurried / hastened' (note the prefix b- in the classical verb). Word order is adverb + direction + verb.
segments.s265-g11: که introduces this clause as explanatory/relative ('for' / 'that'). آمد is past 'came'. سپاهی is the indefinite subject 'an army'. چو + کوه روان is a simile 'like a mountain in motion' — روان here describes motion/flowing, giving the image of a rolling/moving mass.
segments.s265-g12: که again links the next clause. گویی here functions idiomatically and does not match the listed nominal senses (rendered 'as if' in context) — it is best treated as a discourse particle 'it seems/as if' (see senses mapping). ندارند is present plural 'they do not have'. گویا qualifies زبان to mean 'as if they had no (speaking) tongue' — together: 'so that it seemed they had no speech'.
segments.s265-g13: بر آن سان is a fixed phrase 'in that way / in that manner'. که introduces a relative clause 'which' (here: 'which was the custom of a night‑raid'). رسم شبیخون is 'the custom/practice of a night‑raid'. بود is the past copula 'was'.
segments.s265-g14: سپهدار is the commander (subject). داند is the literary present 'knows' (classical narrative often uses present for general knowledge). که introduces the content clause 'that it was thus': آن 'that', چون 'in what way / thus', بود past 'was'.
segments.s265-g15: به لشکر بفرمود is a courteous/literary construction 'he said/commanded to the army' (بفرمود is honorific past). پس here means 'then'. پهلوان is vocative or direct addressee 'O champions / heroes'.
segments.s265-g16: که introduces reported speech/command. بیدار باشید is the 2nd‑person plural subjunctive/imperative 'be awake/alert' (باشید used as an imperative). و simply connects items 'and'. روشن‌روان is an epithet 'bright‑souled / cheerful' describing the state desired of the addressees.
segments.s265-g41: ز ترکان دو بهره فتاده نگون is built as a genitive/partitive phrase + past participle: ز ترکان = “of/from the Turks,” دو بهره = “two shares/portions” (here = two of them/groups), and فتاده نگون is a compound predicate meaning “have fallen/been overthrown” (فتاده, past participle; نگون an adjectival qualifier ‘overthrown/ill‑fated’). Word order places the possessor (ترکان) before the quantity (دو بهره), then the verbal adjective phrase describing their fallen state.
segments.s265-g42: به زیر پی اسب غرقه به خون is an adverbial location/condition phrase. به زیر = “beneath/under,” پی اسب = “the (horse’s) foot/hooves” (پی = foot/track, followed by the genitive اسب), and غرقه به خون is a predicative adjective phrase “drenched in blood” modifying the fallen. Note the second به links the adjective to its complement (‘in/with blood’).
segments.s265-g43: یکی تیر بر اسب نستیهنا: یکی تیر = “one arrow” (indefinite subject), بر marks the point of impact ‘upon/on’, and اسب نستیهنا is a genitive construction ‘the horse of Nastiyehna’ (نستیهنا is an obscure/archaic name/term). The phrase thus says an arrow hit that horse.
segments.s265-g44: رسید از گشاد و بر بیژنا: رسید here is simple past ‘reached/arrived’ used of the arrow’s motion (‘it reached/passed’); از گشاد = ‘through/from the opening/gape’ (از marking passage or source, گشاد ‘gaping/open’), and و بر بیژنا = ‘and upon/against Bižna’ (بر = onto; بیژنا likely a proper name). The clause describes the arrow passing through a gap and hitting the named target.
segments.s265-g45: ز درد اندر آمد تگاور به روی: ز درد = ‘from pain’ (gives the cause), اندر آمد (literally ‘came in’) here functions idiomatically as ‘fell’ or ‘came down’ and تگاور is the subject ‘the rider/warrior.’ به روی = directional/completive phrase ‘onto (his) face.’ So the segment means the rider, stricken with pain, fell forward onto his face.
segments.s265-g46: رسید اندر او بیژن جنگجوی: رسید = ‘came/reached’; اندر او = ‘upon/into him’ (object marked by اندر + pronoun); بیژن جنگجوی follows the verb as the subject in inverted order and identifies who came — ‘Bijan the warrior.’ The whole means ‘Bijan the warrior came upon him.’
segments.s265-g47: عمودی بزد بر سر ترگ‌دار: عمودی = adverb ‘vertically/straight down’ placed before the verb, بزد = ‘he struck’ (past), بر سر = ‘upon the head’ indicating the target, and ترگ‌دار is a post‑positive adjective describing the head (‘scarred/cracked’). The line narrates a downward blow to the cracked head.
segments.s265-g48: تهی ماند از او مغز و برگشت کار: تهی ماند = ‘remained empty’ (i.e., became emptied), از او مغز = a partitive/ablative construction ‘of/from him the brain’ (meaning his brain came out/was emptied), و برگشت کار = ‘and the matter/affair turned (back)’ — برگشت as ‘reversal’ or ‘turning’ of the situation. Word order places the state verb first, then the source phrase, then the conjunctional result.
segments.s265-g97: میان is a preposition meaning 'among' or 'between' and introduces a locative phrase. سپه is the head noun 'army/host'; کاویانی (Kayanian) is an adjectival/tribal epithet modifying سپه. درفش ('banner') is placed after the noun phrase in poetic word order (ezāfe is omitted), functioning as the focal noun ('the Kayanian banner among the host').
segments.s265-g98: به پیش here works as a directional phrase 'in front of / before' (به + پیش). اندرون ('within/inside') narrows the location to 'inside/in its interior'. تیغهای is the plural of تیغ ('sword/blade') and بنفش is the color adjective 'purple'; the phrase describes what stood before the banner. Note the compact, somewhat elliptical poetic order.
segments.s265-g99: همه is the quantifier 'all/every'. نامداران ('renowned ones') is the plural substantive; پرخاشخر is an adjectival epithet 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying نامداران. The three words form a tightly packed noun phrase 'all the famed, quarrelsome ones.'
segments.s265-g100: This short clause lists the weapons associated with those men. ابا here is elliptical/archaic and functions to introduce the instruments (effectively 'with' or 'having'), a usage that is not transparent in the provided sense list (see note). نیزه ('spear') and گرزه ('mace') are the weapons; گاوسر is a proper name in apposition to the mace (i.e. 'the mace called Gausar').
segments.s265-g101: سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial time expression 'at daybreak / dawn'. اندر is a locative/prepositional particle here forming part of the verbal phrase 'اندر آمد' = 'came in'. آمد is the past-tense verb 'came' and سپاه is the subject 'the army', placed after the verb in inverted poetic order.
segments.s265-g102: به پیکار is a prepositional phrase 'to battle / into combat'. تا introduces a result or extent meaning 'until', and گشت is the past tense of 'become/turn'. گیتی ('world') is the subject of گشت and سیاه ('black/dark') is the predicate adjective — 'until the world became dark/black', a poetic way to say the fighting made the day seem dark.
segments.s265-g103: برفتند is the past-tense verb 'they departed/went away'. زان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after/then'). به بنگاه خویش is a destination phrase 'to their ben-gah / place/quarters' — note the compound خوی + ش yields 'their/own', and the precise sense of بنگاه is contextually 'place/quarters' (a slightly archaic usage).
segments.s265-g104: به خیمه شد این، آن به خرگاه خویش is a paired clause: به خیمه شد این = 'this went/was placed to its tent' and آن به خرگاه خویش = 'that (one) to its donkey-stall/lowly place'. به introduces destination; شد here is the past verb 'was/rode/went' in the narrative sense; این و آن are demonstratives indicating respective parties; خرگاه is a lowly stable/donkey-stall and خوی + ش again marks 'their/own.'
segments.s265-g1: دو بهره is a temporal phrase 'two portions/watches' (a time-measure). چَو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the clause. از تیره شب is a genitival/partitive phrase 'of/from the dark night' (از here marks the portion of night). درگذشت is a past-tense intransitive verb 'passed' (classical usage; the numeral phrase is the subject though the verb appears singular).
segments.s265-g2: ز is the poetic variant of از 'from/through/by reason of'. جوش here means the violent surge/tumult caused by the riders. سواران is the nominal agent 'the horsemen'. بجوشید is a past intransitive verb 'boiled/seethed' describing the condition of the dative subject that follows; word order (prepositional phrase before verb, subject after) is typical of epic diction. دشت is the place/subject 'the plain' which 'boiled'.
segments.s265-g3: گرفتند is used idiomatically with an infinitive to mean 'took to / began (doing)' (literally 'they took'). ترکان همه is the nominal phrase 'all the Turks' (subject). تاختن is the infinitive 'to gallop/charge' and completes the verbal idea begun by گرفتند.
segments.s265-g4: بدان functions as a demonstrative prepositional phrase 'by/with that' (classically be + ān = 'with that/at that'); here it links the following infinitive to the previous action ('at that charging'). تاختن is again the infinitive 'charging/galloping'. گردن افراختن is an idiomatic infinitival construction 'to raise/arch the neck' (گردن = neck, افراختن = to raise), describing a proud or attacking posture. Note: the provided split into ب + دان is a lexical segmentation; دان as listed does not match the demonstrative here, so the demonstrative sense is irregular.
segments.s265-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. نستیهن is a proper name (subject). آن لشکر کینه‌خواه is a noun phrase 'that vengeful army/host' with آن as the demonstrative determiner, لشکر the head noun, and کینه‌خواه a postpositive adjective 'vengeful/revenge-seeking'.
segments.s265-g6: بیاورد is the past transitive verb 'he brought'. نزدیک here functions adverbially/locatively 'near' (put before the object in the poetic word order). ایران سپاه is an inverted genitive combination 'Iran's army' (the possessor precedes the possessed in classical verse), so the whole phrase means 'brought that host close to Iran's army.'
segments.s265-g7: سپیده‌دمان is an adverbial/time-word 'at daybreak' (prefixed element marking the time of action). تا indicates extent 'as far as / to' and combines with بدانجا 'to that place' (بدانجا = به + آن‌جا 'to that place'). رسید is the past-tense verb 'reached/arrived.' Word order (time phrase + extent + verb) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s265-g8: چو 'when' introduces a subordinate temporal clause. از marks source 'from'. دیده‌گه is a compound 'lookout / watch-post' (دیده = eye/seeing, گه = place/spot), so از دیده گه means 'from the lookout'. دیده‌بانش is the watchman/sentinel with the possessive suffix -ش 'his' (the watchman belonging to them/their watch); بدید is the past tense 'he saw.' The clause thus reads 'when the watchman saw (from his lookout)'.
segments.s265-g57: چو here introduces a simile ('like/as'); the noun phrase that follows (پیلان همه دشت بر یکدگر) places the plural subject 'pīlān' ('elephants') with modifiers in a compact, somewhat inverted poetic order: 'همه دشت' = 'throughout the plain' and 'بر یکدگر' = 'upon one another'. The sequence is a descriptive simile rather than a full clause with an explicit finite verb.
segments.s265-g58: فگنده is a past participle/adjectival form meaning 'thrown' or 'cast' and here predicates the state ('having been thrown/piled'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تنها جدا مانده is a small compound of participle + adjectives 'left/staying apart, alone' modifying سر ('head'), which appears after it — a common poetic order (modifier before head). Together it says that one head remained apart, having been thrown off/left isolated.
segments.s265-g59: از ... تا به sets up a spatial range 'from ... as far as/to'; آن رزمگه is 'that battlefield' and تا به توران marks the far limit 'as far as Turan'. سپاه (army/host) is the logical subject placed after the range for poetic word order.
segments.s265-g60: دمان (دامان/foothills, 'skirts' of land) with the following locative phrase 'از پس اندر' ('from/behind into') gives the place; گرفتند راه is an SOV-like poetic ordering where گرفتند ('they took') governs راه ('the road/route'): 'they took the road into/among the foothills behind.'
segments.s265-g61: چو here is temporal 'when'; پیران is the subject 'the elders'. ندید is the simple past negative 'did not see' (the object is omitted but implied). آن زمان 'at that time' and با سپاه 'among/with the host' give the temporal and locative circumstances; word order is compact as typical in poetry.
segments.s265-g62: This clause states the emotional result: برادر is 'brother', بدو 'to him'; گشت is the past of گشتن used as 'became/turned' and here governs the following predicate 'گیتی سیاه' ('the world black/dark'). The copular/transformative use 'گشت گیتی سیاه' = 'the world became dark' with بدو indicating for whom.
segments.s265-g63: به + indirect object (کارآگهان) marks 'to the scouts/informants' (those who know); گفت is the reporting verb 'said/told' (poetic use of گفت as the past reportive), and ز این رزمگاه 'from this battlefield' begins the content of what was said. Word order places the recipient and reporting verb before the reported content.
segments.s265-g64: هیونی is a wild/monstrous creature; بتازد is the imperfective/present-stem form meaning 'rushes' or 'charges' (frequentive/poetic present), and به آوردگاه marks the goal 'toward the battlefield/arena'. The clause is a short reported statement: 'a monster rushes toward the field.'
segments.s265-g73: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بشنید is past tense 'heard' (3rd person; Persian past forms do not mark number here). پیران is the subject 'the elders'. برآمد is a past verb 'arose/rose up', and به + جوش (here به as 'into/towards' + جوش 'boiling/surge') is an idiomatic complement: برآمد به جوش = 'was stirred into a boil/rose in a surge'.
segments.s265-g74: نماند is past negative 'did not remain/was not left'. آن زمان = 'at that time' (آن here = 'then/that'). با marks the affected person ('with/regarding the commander'); سپهدار is the title 'commander', and هوش is the noun 'awareness/sense'. The clause thus means that at that moment the commander was left without awareness (word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect).
segments.s265-g75: همی is the iterative/narrative adverb 'kept/continually'. کند is the verbal form of کردن 'does/makes' used here in the narrative to indicate the action (i.e. 'he did/pulled at'). موی is the object 'hair' (so همی کند موی = 'he kept pulling his hair' / 'his hair stood up'). و همی + ریخت آب: the second همی repeats the continuous sense; ریخت with object آب uses the 'pour/flow' sense (i.e. tears or sweat flowed).
segments.s265-g76: از او دور شد is a light-passive/impersonal expression meaning 'departed from him' or 'were removed from him' (از + pronoun + دور شد). خورد و آرام و خواب are a coordinated list of nouns—'eating (appetite), calm (peace), and sleep'—the things that left him. The verb (شد) precedes the list, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s265-g77: بزد (past) 'he struck/struck down'; دست is the instrument/object 'hand' (بزد دست = 'he struck with his hand' or 'he smote his hand'). The coordinating و joins the next past verb بدرید 'tore', which takes رومی قبای as its object ('the Roman's robe' — رومی qualifying قبای). The two verbs share the same subject (understood from context).
segments.s265-g78: برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth'. خروشیدن‌های‌های is a noun denoting loud cries/uproars (the doubled/plural ending is emphatic/poetic). The verb precedes the noun here, so 'cries arose' / 'uproars were raised'.
segments.s265-g79: همی گفت is the iterative narrative 'he kept saying'. کای is the vocative particle 'O' (address). کردگار جهان is the addressee 'Creator (Lord) of the world' (کردگار = Creator/God, جهان = world). Note: the lexical senses supplied for گفت do not include the simple verb 'said', so that entry is not matched below.
segments.s265-g80: همانا 'verily' introduces the assertion; که introduces the subordinate clause. با تو can mean 'with you' / 'in your possession' or 'by you' depending on context. بدستم here uses the sense 'into the hands of / obtained' (i.e. something is in someone's hands), and نهان is 'hidden'. Together the clause expresses that what is hidden is in (or at) your hands — an archaic/poetic ordering where the predicate elements follow.
segments.s265-g105: سپهدار ایران is a title + genitive: head noun 'سپهدار' (commander) followed by the domain 'ایران' (of Iran); the epic style omits an explicit ezāfe. به زیبد رسید is a poetic verbal phrase where به + noun/adjective + رسید (3rd pers. sing. past) expresses 'attained/became' — here 'reached a good state' (i.e. was pleased).
segments.s265-g106: This is a clause in which a cause/phrase (از اندیشه کردن) precedes the affected subject (دلش) and the verb (بردمید). اندیشه کردن is a verbal noun + light verb construction meaning 'to think/worry', دلش = 'his heart' (دل + possessive -ش), and بردمید is an archaic past-form meaning 'took away / was taken away' (here 'was relieved / his heart was lifted').
segments.s265-g107: همی گفت is an archaic/poetic prefective construction in which همی adds an iterative/emphatic aspect before گفت ('he said' — 3rd person past). کامروز is an adverb meaning 'this very day'. رزمی گران is a compound phrase: رزمی (martial) modifies گران (an intensifier/qualifier here meaning 'worthy/important'), together 'men of arms' or 'warriors'.
segments.s265-g108: Two coordinated verbs in 1st person plural past form (بکردیم و کشتیم) are used here in quoted speech with a hortative/future force ('we will accomplish' / 'we shall slay'). و links the verbs. از ایشان سران places the source/target after the verbs: از = 'from/of', ایشان = 'them', سران = 'the chiefs/leaders' (object marked by از).
segments.s265-g109: گمانی functions as a modal/adverbial 'I suppose / perhaps' and is followed by برم, an archaic 1st-person verb-form (here conveying 'I suppose / I think'); زانک = 'because / since', پیران = 'the elders' (plural), کنون = 'now'. Word order places the speaker's conjecture before the reason clause.
segments.s265-g110: دواند here is an older 3rd-person plural/present-past form meaning 'rush/run (they run)' and takes سوی ('toward') as its directional complement. شاه ترکان is a nominal phrase 'the king of the Turks' and هیون is an epithet 'beast/monster' used pejoratively after it; the sequence shows direction → target.
segments.s265-g111: وز او is 'from him' (an older/prepositional use), followed by یار خواهد: یار = 'ally/companion' and خواهد (3rd pers. sing.) here has future/auxiliary sense 'will desire/ask for'. به جنگ سپاه places purpose/role: به = 'for/to', جنگ = 'war', سپاه = 'army' — together 'allies for the army in the war'. The clause reports what they will seek from that person.
segments.s265-g112: رسانم is 1st-person singular (present/near future sense) 'I will deliver / I will bring (a message)'. کنون is the adverb 'now'; آگهی من = 'my news/information' (آگهی = information, من = possessive 'my' placed after the noun in this poetic order); به شاه = 'to the king' (recipient). The verb governs the object and the indirect object in this order typical of Persian.
segments.s265-g81: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that/when' introducing a temporal/relative clause. بگسست is an archaic past (intransitive) form meaning 'was torn/severed' (passive sense). از + noun marks the source ('from'); بازوان زور من is a genitive/possessive sequence ('the arms of my might') with the possessive pronoun من placed after the possessed noun, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s265-g82: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. تیره شد is a two-word predicate: تیره (adjective 'dark/bleak') + شد (past of شدن 'to become'), so 'became dark'. اختر و هور من is the noun phrase 'my star and my splendour' (من as possessive follows and scope extends over both nouns); in poetic word order the subject follows or accompanies the verb phrase.
segments.s265-g83: دریغ is an interjection/exclamation ('alas' / 'pity'). آن is the demonstrative 'that'. هژبر is a proper name or epithet; افگن here is an adjectival epithet meaning 'radiant/shining' appended after the name (a common poetic placement). گردگیر is a compound noun/epithet 'dust-cloak' (a garment or epithet meaning dust-covered), following the same noun phrase.
segments.s265-g84: This is a string of epithets modifying the following name: جوان 'young', دلاور 'valiant/heroic', سوار 'rider/horseman' — all attributive adjectives/nouns preceding the proper name هژیر. Persian poetry freely stacks adjectives and titles before or after names; here they build a descriptive title for Hojir.
segments.s265-g85: گرامی is an adjective 'dear/honoured' placed before برادر 'brother' to form the address 'honoured/dear brother'. جهانبان is an epithet/noun 'world-guardian' or 'protector of the world' that follows and further identifies the addressee. من after جهانبان is the possessive 'my' attached postpositively to the noun phrase (typical in Persian).
segments.s265-g86: سر here means 'head' or 'chief' (leader). ویسگان is a plural/collective 'the people/followers of Vis' (a group name). گرد in the listed senses can mean 'around/about' but in poetry it often participates in compound expressions indicating a retinue/gathering; grammatically this sequence is another appositive/title. هومان is a proper name and من is the possessive 'my' attached to it; the overall phrase strings several honorifics and group-titles together (common in epic diction).
segments.s265-g87: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when/as'. نستیهن is a proper name (Nastīhan). آن شیر شرزه is an appositive epithet: آن 'that', شیر 'lion' (figurative for a brave warrior), شرزه 'proud/insolent' (adjective after the noun, a poetic order). به جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'to/into battle' (به + noun indicating direction or arena).
segments.s265-g88: که here introduces a clause giving what happened in that situation ('that/where'). روباه بودی is an archaic past construction: روباه 'a fox' + بودی (past of بودن, here 'there was / was') — poetic past form. به جنگش is a prepositional phrase 'in his battle' where the possessive clitic -ش attaches to جنگ ('his fight'). پلنگ at the end is the noun 'leopard/panther', placed as a final identifying noun (poetic inversion); overall the line lists contrasting animals as metaphors for opponents in battle.
segments.s265-g17: بخواند is a third-person narrative/past-form used in epic Persian to mean 'he summoned/called'; آن زمان is a temporal adverbial 'at that time'; the names بیژن گیو with را on گیو form the definite direct object (the را after گیو scopes over the name cluster), so the clause reads 'he called Bijan, Giv.'
segments.s265-g18: This is a name/appositive phrase: ابا is here a proper name (not the verbal sense listed), modified by the compound noun تیغ‌زن 'sword‑striker/swordsman'; لشکر نیو 'army of Niu' is a genitive group, and the final را again marks the whole phrase as a definite object in the list of summoned men.
segments.s265-g19: بدو گفت is the ditransitive order 'to him (بدو) he said (گفت)'; what follows ('نیک اختر و کام تو') is the content/blessing: نیک اختر literally 'good/auspicious star' (an idiomatic wish) and کام تو 'your desire/wish' (an abbreviated blessing: 'may your star be good and your wish fulfilled').
segments.s265-g20: This is a nominal comment clause: شکسته‌دل (compound adjective 'broken‑hearted') modifies دشمن 'the enemy'; از نام تو is a causal/agentive phrase 'because of / on account of your name/reputation', so the whole means 'the enemy is broken‑hearted because of your renown.'
segments.s265-g21: ببر is an imperative 'take!' (command), followed by the indefinite object هرک 'whoever'; باید is a modal 'must/ought' indicating necessity; ز گردان من 'from my ranks' is a prepositional phrase where گردان 'troop/rank' takes the possessor من after it (classical word order: noun + possessor).
segments.s265-g22: This continues/specifies the source introduced by از: از این نامداران و مردان من = 'from these illustrious/renowned people and my men'; این 'these' picks out the following nouns, and من again follows the nouns to mark possession ('my').
segments.s265-g23: پذیره شو is an imperative phrase 'be receptive / accept' (پذیره 'acceptance' + شو 'become' used imperatively); این تاختن را is the object 'this charge/assault' with را as definite object marker; چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' modifying the imperative.
segments.s265-g24: سپاه اندر آور is an instruction 'bring/put the army in' (سپاه 'army' + اندر آور 'bring into/subject'); به مردی به زیر is a goal/result phrase 'under a man' (به مردی = 'to/under one man' and به زیر = 'under/beneath'), so together: 'place the army under the authority of one man.'
segments.s265-g65: که here is a purpose/connective ('so that / let him') introducing the intended action. آرد is a third-person form (classical/persuasive subjunctive/present) meaning 'bring' or 'may bring' used to express the wished-for result. نشانی is an object meaning 'a sign/token/news', and ز + نستیهنم = 'from Nesti‑han' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from', نستیهنم is the proper name).
segments.s265-g66: وگرنه is the connective 'otherwise'. دو دیده ز سر is a direct object phrase 'two eyes from (my) head' (word order: numeral + noun + prepositional source). برکنم is first‑person (classical) subjunctive/optative used here as a threat/future: 'I will pluck off' (literally 'may I pluck off' but functioning as 'I will').
segments.s265-g67: هیونی is a collective/pejorative plural 'savage creatures / monsters'. برون + تاختند is a past tense clause: برون (forth/out) + تاختند (3rd pl. 'they rushed/charged'). آن زمان is a temporal phrase 'at that time/then' placed after the verb as is common in classical Persian.
segments.s265-g68: This clause strings three past actions with و: برفت (an archaic/poetic past 'he/it went' — here an irregular/poetic form), بدید 'he saw' (past), و بیامد 'and he came'. دمان 'hem/skirt/edge' is the place reached. Note: the lemma/senses supplied for برفت in the data do not match this verbal use, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.s265-g69: که at the start of this phrase functions as a relative 'who/that'. نستیهن is the proper name. آنک is a classical relative/demonstrative 'who/which' reinforcing the relative clause. ب here is the prepositional prefix (poetic b‑ = 'in/to') combining with the following element to make 'بدان' = 'in that'; دان in the tokenization appears separately but its listed senses don't match this demonstrative use (so we mark that sense -1). رزمگاه is the noun 'battlefield'.
segments.s265-g70: ابا here is a verbal/lexical item meaning 'to be reluctant / to refuse' (an older usage) and heads the clause; it governs the following noun phrase. نامداران is an epithet 'renowned / famed', توران specifies the people/land (Turan), and سپاه is 'host/army'. Word order places the (archaic) verb or verbal notion before the descriptive NP; the whole segment names the famed Turanian host related to Nesti‑han.
segments.s265-g71: بریده is a past participial/adjectival form 'cut off / cut down' describing condition. سرافگنده is an adjective 'ashamed / disgraced'. بر سان is a classical simile construction: بر 'upon / like' + سان 'like/as', so بر سان پیل = 'like an elephant'. Adjectives and simile follow the participial descriptor in this poetic order.
segments.s265-g72: تن is 'body' (here collective/individual bodily condition). از گرز خسته is a causal/prepositional phrase 'weary from the mace' (از = 'from', گرز = 'mace/club', خسته = 'tired'). به کردار is a figurative locution 'in the manner/appearance of' (به as adverbial/marker + کردار 'manner/appearance'), and نیل here is the color 'indigo' — a poetic way to say bruised/dark blue.

sentences.s265-s13: Bijan selected a thousand mounted fighters — brave and combative — from the army.
sentences.s265-s14: Then, on the heights, the two armies — full of hatred and eager for battle — came toward one another.
sentences.s265-s15: All lifted their maces; wholly, a cloud of dark dust closed in (rose).
sentences.s265-s16: A black cloud descended from the mountain and covered the sight of the Turanian army.
sentences.s265-s45: Whom shall I find now in this battlefield, for the army must be brought into battle.
sentences.s265-s46: He sounded the iron flute and struck the drum; the air turned indigo, the earth (became) black as ebony.
sentences.s265-s47: From the mountain, with effort, the host came forth; light arose from the sun and moon.
sentences.s265-s48: The commander of Iran blew the trumpet; he brought the army in and seized the place.
sentences.s265-s17: When the commander saw the dark cloud of dust, from which the Turk army vanished.
sentences.s265-s18: He ordered the bows to be strung; a cry arose from the chiefs, and from that...
sentences.s265-s19: When Bijan entered Nestiḥan, he saw the banner — the chief standard of the Visgān.
sentences.s265-s20: The air had entirely turned rust-colored; the earth became like a sea of blood.
sentences.s265-s25: Thus Bijan said to the Iranians, 'Whoever girds his loins (for the fight)...'
sentences.s265-s26: Except those who take a mace or sword in hand — I will set a bow upon his head and utterly abase him.
sentences.s265-s27: For the Turks, at the sight of a fair (fairy-faced) woman, in war they are completely devoid of skill.
sentences.s265-s28: The Kandavaran took courage; the bird-bringing hosts drew up (their) army.
sentences.s265-s5: When the informants learned the news, they hastened swiftly toward Goudarz.
sentences.s265-s6: For an army came like a moving mountain, so that it seemed they had no speech.
sentences.s265-s7: In the manner that is the custom of a night‑raid, the commander knew that it was so.
sentences.s265-s8: He addressed the army and then to the champions said: 'Be awake and of bright spirit.'
sentences.s265-s21: Two of the Turks fell, overthrown, beneath the horse's feet, drenched in blood.
sentences.s265-s22: One arrow struck the horse of Nastiyehna; it passed through an opening and struck Bij(h)na.
sentences.s265-s23: From the pain the rider fell forward onto his face; Bijan the warrior came upon him.
sentences.s265-s24: He struck straight down on the cracked head; the brain spilled out of him, and the affair turned back.
sentences.s265-s49: Among the host stood the Kayanian banner, and before it, within, purple swords.
sentences.s265-s50: All the famed, quarrelsome champions, with spear and the mace Gausar.
sentences.s265-s51: At daybreak the army came in; they took the field until the world turned black.
sentences.s265-s52: They departed after that to their quarters; this went to its tent, that to its stable.
sentences.s265-s1: When two watches of the dark night had passed, the plain boiled up from the surge of the horsemen.
sentences.s265-s2: All the Turks took to galloping; at that charge they arched their necks.
sentences.s265-s3: When Nastīhan brought that vengeful host near Iran's army.
sentences.s265-s4: At daybreak they reached that place; when the watchman from his lookout saw (them).
sentences.s265-s29: Like elephants, all across the plain they fell upon one another; one head remained apart, left alone.
sentences.s265-s30: From that battlefield as far as Turan, the host took the road into the foothills behind.
sentences.s265-s31: When at that time the elders did not see him among the host, the world grew dark for him.
sentences.s265-s32: He told the scouts that from this battlefield a savage beast was rushing toward the field of battle.
sentences.s265-s37: When the elders heard, they rose up in a surge; at that moment the commander had no sense left.
sentences.s265-s38: He kept pulling/tugging at his hair and water poured (from him); eating, calm, and sleep departed from him.
sentences.s265-s39: He struck with his hand and tore the Roman's robe; cries and shouts arose.
sentences.s265-s40: He kept saying, 'O Creator of the world!' — 'Truly, what is hidden is in your hands.'
sentences.s265-s53: The spahbed (commander) of Iran was pleased, and his heart was freed from anxious thought.
sentences.s265-s54: He said: 'This very day we will do deeds of arms and slay their chiefs.'
sentences.s265-s55: I suppose so, for now the elders are running toward the Turk king, the savage one.
sentences.s265-s56: For from him they will seek allies for the war; I will now carry my news to the king.
sentences.s265-s41: When it was torn from the arms of my strength, thus my star and splendour became dark.
sentences.s265-s42: Alas for that radiant Hojbar, dust-cloaked — young, valiant, mounted Hojir.
sentences.s265-s43: My honoured brother, protector of the world; the head of Vis's men — my Homan.
sentences.s265-s44: When Nastīhan, that bold lion, went to battle — in that fight a fox there was; in his combat a leopard.
sentences.s265-s9: At that time he summoned Bijan, Giv, and Aba, the swordsman of the (Niu) army.
sentences.s265-s10: He said to him, 'May your star be auspicious and your desire fulfilled; the enemy is broken‑hearted because of your name.'
sentences.s265-s11: Take whoever must be taken from my ranks — from these renowned and my men.
sentences.s265-s12: Accept this charge like a lion; bring the army under the control of one man.
sentences.s265-s33: Let him bring a sign from Nesti-han; otherwise I will pluck out both eyes from my head.
sentences.s265-s34: The savage creatures rushed forth at that moment; he went, saw, and came to the hem (edge).
sentences.s265-s35: Nesti‑han — who (was) in that battlefield — (among) the famed warriors of Turan's host.
sentences.s265-s36: Cut down and shamefaced like an elephant; their bodies, weary from the mace, were the color of indigo.
